name: Rock Cycle in a Bag
description: Simulate the rock cycle using crayon shavings as a proxy for rock material. Learners break crayons into small pieces (weathering), compress the pieces tightly in foil (sedimentary rock formation), apply heat with warm water to partially melt and reshape them (metamorphic processes), then fully melt and cool them (igneous rock formation). At each stage, learners observe and record changes in texture, structure, and appearance, connecting the physical transformations to real geological processes.
materials: Heavy books or clamps for compression
learning_objectives: Identify the three rock types and the processes that form them
Demonstrate how heat, pressure, and weathering transform rock material
Connect a hands-on simulation to real geological processes
Record systematic observations at each stage of transformation
variations: Compare results with different crayon brands or colors to model mineral composition variation
Advanced: research real examples of each rock type in your local area
Extension: create a visual diagram of the full rock cycle labeled with process names

name: Build a Watershed Model
description: Crumple a large sheet of paper or aluminum foil to create a three-dimensional landscape with ridges, valleys, and basins. Spray water to observe how it flows downhill along ridges, collects in valleys, and pools in low points. Mark ridge lines (watershed boundaries) and drainage paths. Compare the paper model to real topographic maps of local watersheds. Demonstrates how topography determines water flow and why watersheds matter for water management.
materials: Large sheets of paper or aluminum foil
Topographic maps of local area for comparison
Tray or pan to contain runoff
learning_objectives: Explain how topography determines the direction of water flow
Identify ridge lines as watershed boundaries
Connect a physical model to real topographic maps
Understand why watershed management matters for communities
variations: Add impervious surfaces (tape or plastic wrap) to model urban runoff effects
Use food coloring to trace pollution from a specific point source
Compare models made by different students to see how landscape shape affects drainage

name: Map Your Neighborhood Demographics
description: Walk through your neighborhood and systematically observe and record geographic data: land use types (residential, commercial, industrial, green space), building density, transportation infrastructure, public services, and visible cultural markers. Plot observations on a base map. Calculate approximate percentages of different land uses. Compare your neighborhood map with neighborhoods in different areas of your city or town. Develops field observation skills, mapping practice, and awareness of spatial patterns in human geography.
materials: Base map or aerial photo of neighborhood
learning_objectives: Conduct systematic field observation in a geographic context
Classify land use types and map their spatial distribution
Identify patterns in how human activities organize space
Compare geographic patterns across different neighborhoods
variations: Historical comparison: find old maps or photos and document how the area has changed
Quantitative version: calculate precise land-use percentages using grid overlay method
Digital version: use Google Earth imagery instead of physical walking for remote areas

name: Cultural Exchange Fair Planning
description: Research a culture from a world region different from your own. Create a presentation booth covering the region's physical geography (climate, landforms, water features), how that geography shapes daily life (food, clothing, housing, transportation, livelihoods), cultural expressions (art, music, traditions, festivals), and how the culture has adapted to or modified its environment. Emphasize the geographic reasoning: why do people in this region eat these foods, build these houses, and practice these traditions? The answer is always partly geographic.
learning_objectives: Research a world region's physical and cultural geography
Explain how physical geography shapes cultural practices
Communicate geographic findings through visual and oral presentation
Develop appreciation for cultural diversity through geographic understanding
variations: Food geography version: focus on how climate and soil determine regional cuisines
Architecture version: compare building styles across climate zones and explain differences
Virtual exchange: connect with a class in the studied region via video call

name: Treasure Hunt with Coordinates
description: Set up a coordinate grid on the classroom floor or playground using tape and markers. Hide small objects at specific grid coordinates. Give learners coordinate pairs and challenge them to navigate to the correct locations. Progress from simple letter-number grids (A3, B5) to latitude-longitude style coordinates with compass directions (3N, 2W). For older learners, use actual latitude-longitude coordinates on a local map with GPS devices or smartphone apps. Builds coordinate fluency through play.
materials: GPS device or smartphone (for advanced version)
learning_objectives: Navigate using coordinate pairs on a grid system
Understand how two numbers specify a unique location
Progress from simple grids to geographic coordinate concepts
Develop spatial orientation and directional awareness
variations: Reverse treasure hunt: learners place objects and write coordinate clues for classmates
Outdoor geocaching: use real GPS coordinates to find hidden containers
Digital version: use Google Maps coordinates to identify mystery locations worldwide

name: Design a Map of Your School
description: Measure distances, observe spatial relationships, choose symbols, create a legend, determine scale, and draw a map of the school grounds or building. Include all standard cartographic elements: title, orientation (compass rose), legend, and scale bar. After completion, compare maps from different students. No two will be identical, illustrating that mapping involves choices about what to include, what to emphasize, and how to represent three-dimensional reality in two dimensions. Discuss whose map is 'best' -- and whether that question even has a single answer.
materials: Graph paper or large drawing paper
learning_objectives: Apply standard cartographic elements to an original map
Make deliberate choices about what to include and how to represent it
Understand that mapping involves interpretation, not just recording
Compare spatial representations and discuss trade-offs
variations: Accessibility map: create a map highlighting wheelchair-accessible routes and barriers
Historical map: interview older community members and map how the area looked decades ago
Thematic map: map noise levels, temperature zones, or foot traffic patterns across the school

name: Night Sky Observation Journal
description: Over two to four weeks, observe and record the night sky at the same time and location each clear night. Record the Moon's position, shape, and phase. Identify and track bright planets (they move against the star background). Sketch prominent constellations. Note the direction of sunset and how it shifts over weeks. After the observation period, analyze patterns: the Moon's phase cycle, planetary motion, and the slow rotation of constellations through the seasons. This is the same observational method used by astronomers for millennia -- discovery through patient, systematic watching.
learning_objectives: Observe the night sky systematically over an extended period
Track and record lunar phases through a complete cycle
Identify planets as objects that move against the star background
Connect personal observations to astronomical models of motion
variations: Binocular observation: add binocular targets (Moon craters, Jupiter's moons, star clusters)
Cultural astronomy: research how different cultures interpreted the same sky patterns
Photography: take time-lapse photos showing star trails or Moon phase progression

name: Scale Model of the Solar System
description: Calculate and build a scale model of the solar system using everyday objects. If the Sun is a basketball, Earth is a peppercorn about 26 meters away, and Neptune is a small bead about 780 meters away. Learners calculate object sizes and distances for their chosen scale, select appropriate household items, and physically walk or measure the distances. The emptiness of space becomes visceral when you realize that a scale model that fits the Sun in a gym requires Neptune to be nearly a kilometer away. Develops proportional reasoning and cosmic scale intuition simultaneously.
learning_objectives: Calculate proportional sizes and distances for a scale model
Develop intuition for the vast emptiness of the solar system
Apply mathematical reasoning to astronomical data
Communicate scale relationships using physical models
variations: Classroom scale: fit inner planets in the room, mark outer planets on a campus map
Digital scale: create a to-scale digital diagram and compare with non-scale textbook images
Time scale addition: mark how long light takes to travel between planets at this scale

name: Weather Station Data Collection
description: Set up a simple weather station and collect daily data at the same time for at least two weeks: temperature, cloud cover (estimated percentage and type), precipitation (yes/no and amount), wind direction (using a vane or flag), and wind speed (estimated Beaufort scale). Organize data in tables and create graphs showing trends. Compare personal measurements with official weather service data to assess accuracy. Identify passing weather systems by noting patterns in temperature, pressure, and wind shifts. Teaches systematic data collection, organization, and the observational foundations of meteorology.
materials: Wind vane or ribbon on a stick
learning_objectives: Collect weather data systematically over multiple days
Organize data in tables and create graphs showing trends
Compare personal measurements with official data sources
Identify patterns that indicate passing weather systems
variations: Extended collection: gather data for a full month and analyze longer-term patterns
Cross-location: compare data with a partner in a different climate zone
Forecasting challenge: use collected data patterns to predict tomorrow's weather

name: Climate Zone Research Project
description: Select a climate zone different from your own (tropical, arid, temperate, continental, or polar). Research its temperature and precipitation patterns using real climate data. Create a climate graph (climograph) showing monthly averages. Investigate the typical vegetation, animal life, soil types, and human adaptations found in this zone. Compare and contrast with your home climate zone. Present findings emphasizing the geographic reasoning: how does climate shape the entire living and human landscape of a region? Connects atmospheric science to ecology, agriculture, and human geography.
materials: Climate data sources (weather services, online databases)
Graph paper or graphing software for climographs
learning_objectives: Research and present a climate zone using real data
Create and interpret climographs showing temperature and precipitation patterns
Connect climate patterns to vegetation, wildlife, and human activity
Compare climate zones and explain what causes their differences
variations: Climate change projection: research how your chosen zone is expected to shift under warming
Twin city comparison: compare two cities at the same latitude but different climates and explain why
Historical climate: research how the climate of your region was different during the last ice age
